results.title: C'est l'aviron
results.creator_credit: Norman McLaren and National Film Board of Canada
results.brief_description:
Episode of Series “Chants populaires”.
A short animated version of a French folk song. McLaren used gouache drawings which he superimposed against a scenic background to give the impression of a boat travelling along a lake and river which he acheived by zooming during the photographic process.

results.title: La-haut sur ces montagnes. C'est l'aviron.
results.creator_credit: Jean Palardy, Norman McLaren and National Film Board of Canada
results.brief_description:
Episode of Series “Chants populaires”.
Animated interpretation of two French folk songs. Featuring Madeleine Lachance and the Quatuor Alouette. ‘La-haut sur ces montagnes’ (2.59 mins) is set on the island of Orleans near Quebec, where traditional dances and folksongs are kept alive by the peasant community. In ‘C’est l’aviron’ (3.15 mins) McLaren used gouache drawings which he superimposed against a scenic background to give the impression of a boat travelling along a lake and river which he acheived by zooming during the photographic process.
results.creator_credit_for_label: Jean Palardy, Norman McLaren and National Film Board of Canada

results.title: The Faberge eggs: mementos of a doomed dynasty
results.creator_credit: Barry Stoner, Suzanne Duroux and Stoner Productions
results.brief_description:
Episode of Series “Treasures of the world”.
It was in 1885 that Alexander III approached Peter Carl Faberge to make an Easter gift for his wife - an ornate egg with a surprise inside that would begin a tradition that lasted until the end of the Romanov Empire. Commissioned by the Tsar, Faberge became the royal supplier for Alexander III and continued to supply them to his son, Nicholas, after the death of Alexander in 1894. Through this lucrative connection Faberge received international attention and made his way into many households as the form of a royal gift. Many of the 50 + eggs are discussed in detail as well as put into the context of imperial privilege. Particular emphasis is placed on the relationship between the eggs and the Romanov dynasty, with the fall of the Romanov empire ceasing production of the Faberge eggs, causing Faberge to flee the country and the fate of the precious eggs to be scattered across the world.
results.creator_credit_for_label: Barry Stoner, Suzanne Duroux and Stoner Productions

results.title: Guernica: testimony of war
results.creator_credit: Barry Stoner, Suzanne Duroux and Stoner Productions
results.brief_description:
Episode of Series “Treasures of the world”.
A detailed documentary investigating the genesis and impact of Pablo Picasso’s influential work, Guernica. Picasso was approached to provide a centrepiece for the Spanish pavilion at the Exposition Internationale des Arts et Techniques des la vie Moderne 1937 in Paris. For months he searched for inspiration, plagued by turmoil in his personal life, and the political problems savaging Spain at the time. Then, on April 26th 1937, the Spanish village of Guernica was mercilessly reduced to rubble by tonnes of explosive which killed many. This display of military power, ordered by Franco to break resistance against nationalist forces, left seventy percent of the town destroyed, and attracted a large protest in Paris. Stunned by the stark monochromatic photographs of this destruction, Picasso began to map out the images for what would become his own interpretation of and reaction to this apocalyptic vision of war. The first drafts of the composition, the motifs which begin to take shape, and the final result of this bold political statement on the artists’s behalf are explored in great detail, utilising archival materials and interviews with art historians.
results.creator_credit_for_label: Barry Stoner, Suzanne Duroux and Stoner Productions
results.description:
Episode of Series “Treasures of the world”.
A detailed documentary investigating the genesis and impact of Pablo Picasso’s influential work, Guernica. Picasso was approached to provide a centrepiece for the Spanish pavilion at the Exposition Internationale des Arts et Techniques des la vie Moderne 1937 in Paris. For months he searched for inspiration, plagued by turmoil in his personal life, and the political problems savaging Spain at the time. Then, on April 26th 1937, the Spanish village of Guernica was mercilessly reduced to rubble by tonnes of explosive which killed many. This display of military power, ordered by Franco to break resistance against nationalist forces, left seventy percent of the town destroyed, and attracted a large protest in Paris. Stunned by the stark monochromatic photographs of this destruction, Picasso began to map out the images for what would become his own interpretation of and reaction to this apocalyptic vision of war. The first drafts of the composition, the motifs which begin to take shape, and the final result of this bold political statement on the artists’s behalf are explored in great detail, utilising archival materials and interviews with art historians.
